lineitem.notes: Largest table in TPC-H (~6M rows at SF=1). All queries scan this table with date filters. Prime candidate for parallelization.

orders.notes: Used in Q3 join chain. Moderate size, filtered by o_orderdate.

customer.notes: Small dimension table. Highly selective filter on c_mktsegment in Q3.

part.notes: Not accessed in this query subset but part of schema.

partsupp.notes: Not accessed in this query subset but part of schema.

supplier.notes: Not accessed in this query subset but part of schema.

nation.notes: Not accessed in this query subset but part of schema.

region.notes: Not accessed in this query subset but part of schema.

joins.notes: Build on customer (filtered by mktsegment, ~7% selectivity = ~10K rows), probe with orders. Foreign key join ensures 1:N relationship.
Orders filtered by date, joined with lineitem (also date-filtered). Expected ~4 lineitems per order. Large intermediate result.

filters.notes: Date filter '1998-12-01' - 90 days = Sept 1998. Eliminates ~3 months of data, ~75% selectivity.
One year range (1994). Highly selective, ~14% of data (1 year out of 7 years in TPC-H).
0.05-0.07 range. Narrow band on continuous variable, ~5-10% selectivity.
5 market segments in TPC-H. Single value filter = ~20% selectivity, reduces customer to ~30K rows.

aggregations.notes: Very low cardinality. returnflag: {A, N, R}, linestatus: {O, F}. Combinations = ~4 groups. Ideal for sorted aggregation or thread-local hash tables with trivial merge.
Medium-high cardinality. Groups by orderkey (after filters, ~100K-500K orders remain). Hash aggregation required. Partial aggregation with thread-local tables will be effective.

ordering.notes: Trivial sort of ~4 groups after aggregation. Negligible cost.
Top-K query (LIMIT 10). Can use min-heap or partial sort. Full sort is overkill for large intermediate (100K-500K groups).

optimization_opportunities: Q1: Pure scan-filter-aggregate pipeline. Fuse scan+filter+project into single vectorized loop. Trivial aggregation (4 groups) enables lock-free thread-local hash tables with negligible merge cost.
Q1: Parallelism on lineitem scan. 6M rows / 8 threads = 750K rows/thread. Morsel size ~10K-50K. Expected 7-8x speedup.
Q1: SIMD for filter evaluation (date comparison) and arithmetic (l_extendedprice * (1-l_discount) * (1+l_tax)). 4-8x throughput on AVX2.
Q1: Columnar storage critical. Only 8 of 16 columns accessed. Row storage wastes 50% memory bandwidth.
Q6: Similar to Q1 but with 4 filter predicates (shipdate, discount, quantity). Fuse all filters into single pass. Highly selective (combined selectivity ~0.7%).
Q6: Scan-filter-aggregate pipeline, no joins. Simpler than Q1. Parallelism + SIMD + operator fusion will dominate performance.
Q3: Join ordering critical. Build hash table on customer (filtered to ~30K rows by mktsegment), probe with orders (~600K rows after date filter), then join with lineitem.
Q3: Partitioned hash join on customer-orders. Hash-partition both tables by custkey, join partitions independently across threads. Near-linear scaling.
Q3: Push date filters down to base scans before joins. o_orderdate < '1995-03-15' and l_shipdate > '1995-03-15' reduce input sizes by 40-60% before join.
Q3: Partial aggregation after join. Thread-local hash tables for grouping, then merge. Reduces contention during aggregation phase.
Q3: Top-K optimization for LIMIT 10. Use min-heap to track top 10 by revenue during aggregation, avoid full sort of 100K-500K groups.
Q3: Multi-column GROUP BY (l_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority). Composite key hashing required. Use fast hash functions (xxHash, CRC32).
All queries: Zone maps on date columns (l_shipdate, o_orderdate). TPC-H data is temporally clustered. Skip entire morsels that fall outside date ranges.
All queries: Dictionary encoding for low-cardinality string columns (l_returnflag, l_linestatus, c_mktsegment). Reduces memory footprint and enables integer comparisons.
All queries: Persistent columnar storage with mmap. Eliminates CSV parsing overhead (10-100x faster data loading).

parallelism_opportunities.rationale: Q1, Q3, Q6 all scan lineitem with filters. Morsel-driven parallelism: 6M rows / 10K morsel = 600 morsels. 8 threads achieve ~7-8x speedup (near-linear). Memory bandwidth unlikely to saturate at 6M rows.
Q1 aggregates ~4.5M rows (after date filter) into 4 groups. Thread-local hash tables (8 threads) + merge phase. Merge cost negligible due to low cardinality. Expected 7-8x speedup.
Q3 scans 1.5M orders with date filter. Parallel scan with 8 threads = ~190K rows/thread. Expected 6-7x speedup (slight overhead from smaller data size).

Q3 builds hash table on customer (filtered to ~30K rows). Small table, but partitioning across threads reduces contention during hash table build. Probe phase (orders->customer join) parallelized across orders partitions.

Q3 joins lineitem (~3.6M rows after date filter) with orders. Partitioned hash join: hash-partition lineitem by l_orderkey, join each partition independently. 8 threads = ~450K rows/thread. Expected 7-8x speedup.

Q3 aggregates join result (~400K rows) by orderkey. Thread-local partial aggregation during join reduces global merge cost. Expected 5-6x speedup (merge overhead slightly higher due to medium cardinality).

column_co_access_patterns.storage_hint: Store adjacent in columnar layout for cache-friendly access during aggregation.

column_co_access_patterns.access_pattern: Q1, Q3 compute expressions involving all three
column_co_access_patterns.storage_hint: Store in column group. All three loaded together for arithmetic operations.
Composite key hashing. Pre-compute hash during scan if key is stable.

column_co_access_patterns.access_pattern: Q6 filters on all three simultaneously
column_co_access_patterns.storage_hint: Store in column group for fused filter evaluation. SIMD-friendly layout.

Q1.notes: Simple pipeline. Parallelism + vectorization + operator fusion will achieve near-optimal performance.

Q3.notes: Most complex query. Join ordering, partitioned hash join, and partial aggregation are critical. Top-K optimization for LIMIT 10 provides additional 2-5x speedup.

Q6.notes: Simplest query. Single-table scan with high selectivity filters. Parallelism + SIMD + fused filter sufficient.

lineitem.skew: Minimal. Uniformly distributed orderkeys and dates in TPC-H benchmark.
lineitem.clustering: Ordered by (l_orderkey, l_linenumber). Date columns (l_shipdate, l_commitdate, l_receiptdate) are temporally clustered, enabling zone map pruning.

orders.skew: Minimal. Uniform distribution of customers and dates.
orders.clustering: Ordered by o_orderkey. o_orderdate is monotonically increasing, enabling zone map pruning.

customer.clustering: No clustering in base TPC-H schema.

summary: This TPC-H subset features two scan-intensive queries (Q1, Q6) and one join-heavy query (Q3). Q1 and Q6 are scan-filter-aggregate pipelines on lineitem (6M rows) with highly selective date filters and trivial/no aggregation. Parallelism (8 threads, morsel-driven) combined with SIMD and operator fusion will yield 7-8x speedup. Q3 is the critical path: 3-way join (customer-orders-lineitem) requiring optimal join ordering (build on filtered customer, cascade to orders, then lineitem), partitioned hash joins, and partial aggregation. Columnar storage is essential (only 30-50% of columns accessed per query). Zone maps on date columns enable block-level pruning. Dictionary encoding for low-cardinality columns (returnflag, linestatus, mktsegment) reduces memory bandwidth. Top-K optimization for Q3 LIMIT 10 avoids sorting 100K-500K groups. Expected end-to-end query times at SF=1 with full optimization: Q1 ~500ms, Q3 ~2-3s, Q6 ~300ms (vs 3-4s, 15-20s, 2s single-threaded baseline).
